name: Speculative Decoding
oneliner: Small draft model proposes K candidate tokens, large model verifies all K in one forward pass. 2-3$\times$ speedup with provably identical output distribution.
explanation: Speculative decoding exploits the fact that LLM inference is memory-bandwidth-bound, so processing one token or several tokens in parallel costs nearly the same. A small draft model rapidly generates several candidate tokens, then the large target model verifies all of them in a single forward pass using a modified rejection sampling scheme. Accepted tokens match the target model's distribution exactly, so the output is mathematically identical to standard generation. This typically achieves a two to three times speedup with acceptance rates of 70 to 90 percent.
fundamentals: Verification per position i: accept draft token x with prob min(1, p(x)/q(x)) where p=target, q=draft. If rejected: sample from p'(x) = max(0, p(x)-q(x)) / Z. Expected tokens per step: (1-$\alpha$^(K+1))/(1-$\alpha$) where $\alpha$=acceptance rate. For K=5, $\alpha$=0.8: ~3.36 tokens/step → ~2.7$\times$ speedup. Draft model can be: smaller same-family model, separate small model, or even n-gram model. Self-speculative: use target model with early exit for drafting.
sources: Leviathan et al., 'Fast Inference from Transformers via Speculative Decoding,' ICML 2023, arXiv:2211.17192
Chen et al., 'Accelerating LLM Decoding with Speculative Sampling,' arXiv:2302.01318

foundational_papers.title: Fast Inference from Transformers via Speculative Decoding
Accelerating Large Language Model Decoding with Speculative Sampling
